source_intake_record.raw_question_text:
Hint: Please answer the question requiring a floating-point number with one decimal place and provide the final value, e.g., 1.2, 1.3, 1.4, at the end.
Question: This problem is related to a 'coil gun' problem we will be doing with the freshmen this year. The situation is shown in Figure 1. A permanent magnet can move through a plastic tube. For our purposes we will assume the tube is frictionless. A coil of wire is wrapped around the tube.
In an experiment, the permanent magnet is made to move through the tube with a velocity of $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{S}$. The coil is open circuited and its voltage is measured to be approximately what is shown in Figure 2.
As it turns out, that voltage may be expressed in the form:
$$
v(t)=A t e^{-a t}
$$
where $A=1000 e \approx 2,718 \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{s}$ and $\alpha=500 \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ Note that, in the figure and in this expression we set $t=0$ to the point in time at which the magnet is right in the center of the coil, which is when the voltage goes through zero.
The magnet weighs 50 grams. The magnet starts at at x = 1cm when the coil current
is stepped from zero to some value I_0. The objective is to shoot the magnet to a height
of 1 meter. What value should I_0 have? I_0=( )A

The prompt provides a measured/open-circuit voltage model, initial position, mass, and target height, indicating multiple quantities must be related rather than directly read off
This is an open-ended numeric problem with no choices, so solving likely requires derivation rather than recognition

registry_passed=true and both question and answer are present
The prompt specifies concrete parameters such as A, a, magnet mass, initial position, and target height, enabling analytic or tolerance-based checking of a derived result

normalization_record.normalized_question_text:
This problem is related to a 'coil gun' problem we will be doing with the freshmen this year. The situation is shown in Figure 1. A permanent magnet can move through a plastic tube. For our purposes we will assume the tube is frictionless. A coil of wire is wrapped around the tube.

In an experiment, the permanent magnet is made to move through the tube with a velocity of $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{S}$. The coil is open circuited and its voltage is measured to be approximately what is shown in Figure 2.

As it turns out, that voltage may be expressed in the form:
$$
v(t)=A t e^{-a t}
$$
where $A=1000 e \approx 2,718 \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{s}$ and $\alpha=500 \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. Note that, in the figure and in this expression we set $t=0$ to the point in time at which the magnet is right in the center of the coil, which is when the voltage goes through zero.

The magnet weighs 50 grams. The magnet starts at x = 1 cm when the coil current is stepped from zero to some value $I_0$. The objective is to shoot the magnet to a height of 1 meter. What value should $I_0$ have? $I_0=(\ )\mathrm{A}$
normalization_notes: Removed answer-format hint as packaging noise.
Preserved figure references without inferring additional image details.
Set requires_image=true because force_requires_image=true and the prompt explicitly depends on Figure 1 and Figure 2.

clean_problem_record.normalized_question_text:
This problem is related to a 'coil gun' problem we will be doing with the freshmen this year. The situation is shown in Figure 1. A permanent magnet can move through a plastic tube. For our purposes we will assume the tube is frictionless. A coil of wire is wrapped around the tube.

In an experiment, the permanent magnet is made to move through the tube with a velocity of $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{S}$. The coil is open circuited and its voltage is measured to be approximately what is shown in Figure 2.

As it turns out, that voltage may be expressed in the form:
$$
v(t)=A t e^{-a t}
$$
where $A=1000 e \approx 2,718 \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{s}$ and $\alpha=500 \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. Note that, in the figure and in this expression we set $t=0$ to the point in time at which the magnet is right in the center of the coil, which is when the voltage goes through zero.

The magnet weighs 50g. The magnet starts at x = 1cm when the coil current is stepped from zero to some value $I_0$. The objective is to shoot the magnet to a height of 1m. What value should $I_0$ have? $I_0=(\ )\mathrm{A}$

where $A=1000 e \approx 2,718 \mathrm{v} / \mathrm{s}$ and $\alpha=500 \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ Note that, in the figure and in this expression we set $t=0$ to the point in time at which the magnet is right in the center of the coil, which is when the voltage goes through zero.
The magnet weighs 50 grams. The magnet starts at at x = 1cm when the coil current
is stepped from zero to some value I_0. The objective is to shoot the magnet to a height
of 1 meter. What value should I_0 have? I_0=( )A
